what_it_does: Implements a global vanilla toast notification utility (MS_TOAST.show) and a curated map of button-label-to-confirmation-message strings (BTN_FEEDBACK) intended to give principal actions believable feedback.
observations: Defines window.MS_TOAST as an IIFE exposing a single show(message, opts) method.
ensure() lazily creates a single ms-toast-host container appended to document.body and reuses it while it remains attached.
show() builds a toast div with role="status", an optional inline SVG checkmark, and an HTML-escaped message span.
Toasts auto-dismiss after a configurable duration (default 2800 ms) by adding ms-toast--out, then remove after 260 ms.
The host is removed and nulled only after the last toast child is removed.
Defines window.BTN_FEEDBACK, a dictionary mapping action labels like 'Export', 'Publish update', and 'Run now' to plausible confirmation strings.
Comments state the shared <Btn> should fall back to BTN_FEEDBACK only when it has no real onClick handler.
standouts: Curated, action-specific confirmation lexicon rather than generic success text.
Auto-cleanup of the toast host when empty, keeping the DOM clean.
Vanilla, globally callable API designed to be invoked from shared button components.
conventions: IIFE module attached to window for global access.
HTML entity escaping for user-supplied message text.
CSS-driven exit transition triggered by adding a modifier class before removal.
concerns: innerHTML is used to assemble toast content; while the message is escaped and the SVG is a constant, it is still a string-assembly approach.
esc() only escapes &, <, and >, leaving quotes and apostrophes unescaped, which could matter if the message is later injected into attribute contexts.
The fallback-to-BTN_FEEDBACK logic is described in comments but not implemented in this file, so coupling depends on external button code.
Class name fb-toast appears alongside ms-toast prefixes without explanation in this file.
notes: A self-contained toast renderer plus a curated confirmation phrasebook for unhandled principal actions.
questions: Where is the CSS that defines ms-toast-host, ms-toast, and ms-toast--out transitions?
How does the shared Btn component decide to call MS_TOAST.show versus using BTN_FEEDBACK?
Are toast messages intended to be user-facing in production or only for prototype demos?
Why is the class fb-toast used alongside the ms-toast namespace?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file both implements a lightweight global toast and supplies a curated confirmation map for principal actions.
intention_signals: To make clickable actions feel responsive and credible by showing context-specific confirmations, while staying silent for buttons that would open unimplemented UI.
novelty: A label-keyed phrasebook of believable confirmations tied to the idea of 'only fake feedback when there is no real handler'.
contribution: A reusable, self-cleaning toast renderer and a curated action-label-to-message lexicon that can supply plausible feedback for unhandled principal buttons.
summary_for_embedding: A vanilla JavaScript file that exposes a global MS_TOAST.show utility and a BTN_FEEDBACK map of action labels to realistic confirmation messages for prototype action feedback.
intention_for_embedding: To give principal UI actions immediate, believable toast feedback without wiring every button individually.

symbols.does: Lazily creates and returns the toast host container in document.body.
symbols.description: ensure checks whether an existing host div is still in the DOM; if not, it creates a new div with class ms-toast-host, appends it to document.body, and caches it for reuse.

symbols.does: Escapes &, <, and > in a string for safe HTML insertion.
symbols.description: esc converts the input to a string and replaces ampersands, less-than, and greater-than characters with their HTML entities; it does not escape quotes or apostrophes.

symbols.does: Renders a toast notification with an optional icon and auto-dismisses it.
symbols.description: show builds a div with role status, injects an optional inline SVG checkmark and an escaped message span, appends it to the host, and schedules a CSS exit transition and removal after the configured duration, cleaning up the host when no toasts remain.
